description:
# Philosophy

The SKILL.md today *mentions* not modifying files in the main repo — line 120, buried in an Important bullet list — but it reads like one rule among many. For the 95% of users who let the bl interface govern their agents end-to-end, this should be the load-bearing premise of the document, not a footnote.

The right framing: **balls is the tool, and the correct way to use the tool is that modifications never happen against the operating branch.** Worktrees aren't a convenience flag, they are how the system works. An agent that edits files directly on main while \"using\" balls is misusing it — the task tracker is keeping score of work that happened outside its lifecycle, and the merge/review/close primitives stop being meaningful.

This is slightly looser than my AGENTS.md (which is for my own agents and is unconditional: \"never make file modifications in a repo directly in the repo core's working branch\"), because someone else's agent in someone else's repo isn't mine to govern. But the skill should communicate the philosophy clearly enough that a downstream agent reading SKILL.md walks away understanding: *while using balls to track tasks, modifications go through worktrees and merge in. Editing the operating branch directly defeats the tool.*

# Why this shape

1. The default-flow section already establishes \"one agent finishes the task end-to-end.\" The natural companion principle is \"and the work happens in the worktree, not on main.\" Both are about the lifecycle being a contract, not a suggestion.

2. Today's wording (\"Don't modify files in the main repo while working on a claimed task\") is reactive — it tells you what not to do once you've already claimed. The replacement should be proactive — frame the worktree as where work *belongs*, so the question \"can I just edit this on main real quick\" doesn't even form.

3. The rule should travel with a *why*, not just a prohibition. Editing on main while a task is claimed produces: untracked drift the worktree doesn't see; merge surprises at `bl review` time; a delivery commit that doesn't actually contain all the work; and ambiguity about what the task delivered. State the consequences once and the rule explains itself.

# Scope

- Promote the worktree principle from a buried bullet to a top-level section near the default-flow section, framed as \"how balls works,\" not \"a thing to avoid.\"
- Reword existing references in the Workflow / Important block to point at the new section instead of restating.
- Tone: declarative and confident (\"work happens in the worktree\"), not hedged. Slightly looser than AGENTS.md — no \"never\" on modifying main outside of a claimed-task context, since users are entitled to edit their own repos when they aren't using balls. The principle binds the *balls workflow*, not the user.
- Keep the document's existing voice and density. This is a tightening, not a rewrite.

# Out of scope

- No code or binary changes; SKILL.md only.
- Don't add a new Important-style bullet list. Prefer prose that explains the philosophy.
- Don't duplicate content from the user's private AGENTS.md verbatim — the skill should reflect the philosophy in its own words, suited to a general audience.

# Done when

- A new or significantly expanded section near the top of SKILL.md frames worktree-isolated work as the way the tool is used.
- Downstream references in Workflow and Important point at it cleanly without restating.
- A reader who skims only the first third of the doc still walks away with the principle.
title: Tighten SKILL.md philosophy: worktree-or-bust on the operating branch
